content_id:
Takdir ilahi (al-qadha wal-qadar) menempati bagian yang panjang dalam al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah, mencerminkan kepentingannya yang sentral dalam teologi Islam dan kontroversi-kontroversi historis yang mengelilinginya. Pembahasan al-Tahawi tentang topik ini termasuk yang paling bernuansa dalam teksnya yang ringkas, dengan cermat menavigasi antara dua ekstrem yang aktif pada masanya dan tetap relevan untuk diskusi teologis hari ini.

Al-Tahawi menyatakan: 'Akar takdir ilahi — baik dalam penegasannya maupun dalam kehalusan hakikatnya — termasuk rahasia Allah yang paling tersembunyi dan perkara-Nya yang paling terjaga. Tidak ada malaikat yang didekatkan maupun nabi yang diutus yang dapat memahaminya sepenuhnya. Mendalaminya untuk menentangnya menyebabkan kerugian dan merupakan jalan menuju kehilangan dan kesesatan.' Pernyataan pembuka ini sendiri sangat instruktif: al-Tahawi mengakui kesulitan filosofis dari topik ini dan menyarankan kerendahan hati epistemologis. Takdir ilahi bukanlah masalah matematika yang harus dipecahkan tetapi misteri ilahi yang harus diyakini melalui wahyu dan dipercayai melalui iman.

Doktrin yang ia tegaskan terdiri dari empat elemen yang saling terkait — hal yang sama yang kemudian disistematisasi oleh ulama-ulama berikutnya: ilmu ilahi (bahwa Allah mengetahui semua yang akan terjadi sebelum terjadi), penulisan ilahi (bahwa Allah menuliskan semua peristiwa dalam Lauh Mahfuzh), kehendak ilahi (bahwa tidak ada sesuatu yang terjadi kecuali dengan kehendak-Nya), dan penciptaan ilahi (bahwa Allah adalah Pencipta segalanya, termasuk tindakan-tindakan makhluk-Nya). Keempat elemen ini bersama-sama membentuk pemahaman Sunni tentang kedaulatan ilahi atas seluruh eksistensi.

Implikasi praktis yang al-Tahawi ambil dari ini adalah bahwa kedua ekstrem — kaum Jabriyyah (determinis ekstrem) dan kaum Qadariyyah (mereka yang mengingkari pengetahuan ilahi dan penciptaan tindakan manusia) — adalah keliru. Kaum Jabriyyah menggunakan takdir ilahi sebagai dalih atas dosa: 'Allah menetapkan ini, jadi aku tidak bisa berbuat lain.' Al-Tahawi merespons bahwa hal ini menghapus tanggung jawab moral dan bertentangan dengan kesaksian langsung kesadaran manusia, yang merasakan pilihan yang nyata meskipun pilihan itu berada dalam kerangka takdir ilahi. Sementara itu kaum Qadariyyah mencoba melindungi tanggung jawab manusia dengan membatasi kedaulatan ilahi — sebuah pendirian yang memperkenalkan ke dalam teologi Islam domain eksistensi di luar pengetahuan dan kehendak Allah, yang secara filosofis tidak koheren dan secara teologis tidak dapat diterima.

Al-Tahawi juga membahas pertanyaan mengapa Allah menetapkan kebaikan dan keburukan, ketaatan dan kemaksiatan. Responsnya mencerminkan doktrin Sunni bahwa Allah tidak ditanya tentang apa yang dilakukan-Nya — 'Dia tidak ditanya tentang apa yang dilakukan-Nya, tetapi mereka yang akan ditanya' (QS. al-Anbiya': 23) — dan bahwa kebijaksanaan manusia tidak dapat sepenuhnya memahami kebijaksanaan ilahi. Yang dapat diketahui manusia adalah bahwa Allah itu adil, bahwa Dia tidak menzalimi siapa pun, dan bahwa setiap ketetapan-Nya disertai hikmah meskipun hikmah itu tersembunyi dari kita. Seorang mukmin merespons realitas ini bukan dengan ketidakpuasan tetapi dengan kepercayaan: 'Allah — Mahasuci Dia — lebih penyayang kepada hamba-Nya daripada seorang ibu kepada anaknya.'
content_en:
Divine decree (al-qada wal-qadar) occupies a lengthy section in al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah, reflecting its central importance in Islamic theology and the historical controversies that have surrounded it. Al-Tahawi's treatment of this subject is among the most nuanced in his concise text, carefully navigating between two extremes that were active in his era and remain relevant to theological discussion today.

Al-Tahawi states: 'The root of divine decree — both its affirmation and the subtlety of its nature — is among the most veiled of Allah's secrets and the most closely guarded of His matters. There is no angel brought near nor prophet sent who can comprehend it fully. Delving into it to oppose it leads to deprivation and is a path to loss and deviation.' This opening statement is itself deeply instructive: al-Tahawi acknowledges the philosophical difficulty of the subject and counsels epistemological humility. Divine decree is not a mathematical problem to be solved but a divine mystery to be affirmed through revelation and trusted through faith.

The doctrine he affirms consists of four interconnected elements — the same four that later scholars would systematize: divine knowledge (that Allah knew all that would happen before it happened), divine inscription (that Allah wrote all events in the Preserved Tablet), divine will (that nothing happens except what He wills), and divine creation (that Allah is the Creator of all actions, including those of created beings). Together, these four elements constitute the Sunni understanding of divine sovereignty over all of existence.

The practical implication that al-Tahawi draws from this is that both extremes — the Jabriyyah (extreme determinists) and the Qadariyyah (those who denied divine foreknowledge and creation of human actions) — are in error. The Jabriyyah used divine decree as an excuse for sin: 'Allah decreed this, so I could not have done otherwise.' Al-Tahawi responds that this eliminates moral responsibility and contradicts the direct testimony of human consciousness, which experiences genuine choice even if that choice is within the framework of divine decree. The Qadariyyah, meanwhile, attempted to protect human responsibility by limiting divine sovereignty — a position that introduces into Islamic theology a domain of existence outside Allah's knowledge and will, which is both philosophically incoherent and theologically unacceptable.

Al-Tahawi also addresses the question of why Allah decrees good and evil, obedience and disobedience. His response reflects the Sunni doctrine that Allah is not questioned about what He does — 'He will not be asked about what He does, but they will be asked' (Quran 21:23) — and that human wisdom cannot fully comprehend divine wisdom. What humans can know is that Allah is just, that He does not wrong anyone, and that every decree of His is accompanied by wisdom even when that wisdom is hidden from us. The believer responds to this reality not with resentment but with trust: 'Allah — perfect is He — is more merciful to His servant than a mother is to her child.'
content_ar: Divine decree (al-القضاء wal-القدر) occupies a lengthy الفصل in al-العقيدة al-Tahawiyyah, reflecting its المحوري importance in Islamic theology and the historical controversies that have surrounded it. Al-Tahawi's treatment of this subject is among the most nuanced in his concise text, carefully navigating between two extremes that were active in his era and remain relevant to theological discussion today. Al-Tahawi states: 'The root of divine decree — both its affirmation and the subtlety of its nature — is among the most veiled of الله's secrets and the most closely guarded of His matters. There is no angel brought near nor النبي sent who can comprehend it fully. Delving into it to oppose it leads to deprivation and is a الطريق to loss and deviation.' This opening statement is itself deeply instructive: al-Tahawi acknowledges the philosophical difficulty of the subject and counsels epistemological التواضع. Divine decree is not a mathematical problem to be solved but a divine mystery to be affirmed through الوحي and trusted through الإيمان. The doctrine he affirms consists of four interconnected elements — the same four that later العلماء would systematize: divine العلم (that الله knew all that would happen قبل it happened), divine inscription (that الله wrote all events in the Preserved Tablet), divine will (that nothing happens except what He wills), and divine creation (that الله is the Creator of all actions, بما في ذلك those of created beings). Together, these four elements constitute the Sunni الفهم of divine sovereignty over all of existence. The practical implication that al-Tahawi draws from this is that both extremes — the Jabriyyah (extreme determinists) and the Qadariyyah (those who denied divine foreknowledge and creation of human actions) — are in error. The Jabriyyah used divine decree as an excuse for الذنب: 'الله decreed this, لذا I could not have done otherwise.' Al-Tahawi responds that this eliminates moral المسؤولية and contradicts the direct testimony of human consciousness, which experiences genuine choice even إذا that choice is within the framework of divine decree. The Qadariyyah, meanwhile, attempted to protect human المسؤولية by limiting divine sovereignty — a position that introduces into Islamic theology a domain of existence outside الله's العلم and will, which is both philosophically incoherent and theologically unacceptable. Al-Tahawi أيضاً addresses the question of why الله decrees الخير and الشر, obedience and disobedience. His response reflects the Sunni doctrine that الله is not questioned about what He does — 'He will not be asked about what He does, but they will be asked' (القرآن الكريم 21:23) — and that human الحكمة cannot fully comprehend divine الحكمة.
